content.problemStatement:
Chef loves single-colored squares, so he got a rectangular board with size N×MN \times MN×M consisting only of black cells and white cells. He calculates the cost of the board in the following manner:


Let's denote the number of ways to choose k2k^2k2 white cells which form a square with edge length kkk (and edges parallel to the borders of the board) by WkW_kWk​. For example, a 2×32 \times 32×3 board consisting of 6 white cells and no black cells has W1=6W_1=6W1​=6, W2=2W_2=2W2​=2 and W3=0W_3=0W3​=0.
Similarly, let's denote the number of ways to choose k2k^2k2 black cells forming a square by BkB_kBk​.
Chef chose two sequences of non-negative integers CWC_WCW​ and CBC_BCB​, each with length min(N,M)\mathrm{min}(N, M)min(N,M). The cost of the board is defined as
∑i=1min(N,M)CB,i⋅Bi+CW,i⋅Wi .\sum_{i=1}^{\mathrm{min}(N, M)} C_{B, i} \cdot B_i + C_{W, i} \cdot W_i\,.∑i=1min(N,M)​CB,i​⋅Bi​+CW,i​⋅Wi​.
However, Chef accidentally spilled some sauce on the board while cooking, so the colors of some cells cannot be determined. Now he wants to repaint each of these cells using black or white paint. What is the maximum achievable cost of the resulting board? Note that he may paint different cells using different colors.
constraints: each string contains only characters '0', '1' and '?'
content.inputFormat: The first line of the input contains a single integer T denoting the number of test cases. The description of T test cases follows. The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers N and M. N lines follow. For each i (1≤i≤N), the i-th of these lines contains a string with length M describing the i-th row of the board — the character '0' stands for a white cell, '1' stands for a black cell and '?' stands for a cell with unknown color. The following line contains K=min(N,M) space-separated integers CW,1,CW,2,…,CW,K. The last line contains K=min(N,M) space-separated integers CB,1,CB,2,…,CB,K.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing one integer — the maximum possible cost.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Explanation: Replacing each '?' by '1' gives the optimal answer.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use dynamic programming to calculate the maximum achievable cost of the resulting board. We will iterate over all possible values of k from 1 to min(N,M) and calculate the number of ways to choose k^2 white cells and k^2 black cells that form a square. We will then use these values to calculate the maximum achievable cost.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * N * M * min(N, M)) — explain: per query is O(N * M * min(N, M)), total is O(T * N * M * min(N, M))
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N * M) — explain what dominates: the space complexity is dominated by the space required to store the board
